microprocessor-8085.question: The Intel 8085 is a:
microprocessor-8085.explanation: The 8085 processes data 8 bits at a time — its data bus and accumulator are 8 bits wide.

microprocessor-8085.question: The address bus of the 8085 is 16 bits wide, so it can address a maximum memory of:
microprocessor-8085.explanation: 2¹⁶ = 65,536 distinct addresses = 64 KB of memory locations.

microprocessor-8085.question: In the 8085, the accumulator is register:
microprocessor-8085.explanation: Register A is the accumulator — the primary register for arithmetic and logical operations, holding one operand and the result.

microprocessor-8085.question: Which of the following is NOT a flag in the 8085 flag register?
microprocessor-8085.explanation: The 8085 has five flags: Sign, Zero, Auxiliary Carry, Parity and Carry. A separate overflow flag appears in later processors like the 8086.

microprocessor-8085.question: The Program Counter (PC) of the 8085 holds:
options: the address of the next instruction to be fetched
the result of the last ALU operation
the top of the stack data
microprocessor-8085.explanation: The 16-bit PC always points to the memory address of the next instruction, incrementing automatically as instructions are fetched.

microprocessor-8085.question: The Stack Pointer (SP) in the 8085 is:
options: a 16-bit register holding the address of the top of the stack
a memory location outside the address space
microprocessor-8085.explanation: SP is a 16-bit special-purpose register that stores the address of the most recently pushed item in the stack area of memory.

microprocessor-8085.question: The ALU of the 8085 performs:
microprocessor-8085.explanation: The Arithmetic and Logic Unit carries out operations such as addition, subtraction, AND, OR, XOR and comparisons.

microprocessor-8085.question: In the 8085, the lower-order address bus (A₀–A₇) is multiplexed with:
microprocessor-8085.explanation: Lines AD₀–AD₇ carry the low byte of the address first, then serve as the data bus — demultiplexed using the ALE signal.

microprocessor-8085.question: Which register pair in the 8085 is most often used as a memory pointer (M register)?
microprocessor-8085.explanation: The H-L pair holds a 16-bit memory address; the memory location it points to can be accessed as the pseudo-register M.

microprocessor-8085.question: The HLT instruction of the 8085:
options: stops instruction execution until a reset or interrupt
transfers control to the monitor program
microprocessor-8085.explanation: HLT halts the CPU — the buses go into a high-impedance state and execution stops until an interrupt or reset occurs.

networking-technology.question: A network that connects computers within a small area such as one building is called a:
networking-technology.explanation: A Local Area Network covers a limited area like an office, lab or campus building, typically with high speed and low cost per node.

networking-technology.question: In which topology are all nodes connected to a single central device (hub/switch)?
networking-technology.explanation: In a star topology every node has its own link to the central hub or switch; failure of one link affects only that node.

networking-technology.question: The OSI reference model has how many layers?
networking-technology.explanation: The OSI model defines 7 layers: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation and Application.

networking-technology.question: The protocol suite on which the Internet is based is:
networking-technology.explanation: Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol is the foundational set of communication protocols of the Internet.

networking-technology.question: The device used to connect two different networks and route packets between them is a:
networking-technology.explanation: A router works at the network layer, choosing paths and forwarding packets between distinct networks; hubs and repeaters merely extend a single network.

networking-technology.question: Which of the following is an unguided (wireless) transmission medium?
networking-technology.explanation: Guided media carry signals along a physical cable; radio waves travel through free space and are therefore unguided.

networking-technology.question: An IPv4 address is:
networking-technology.explanation: IPv4 addresses are 32-bit numbers, usually written as four decimal octets like 192.168.0.1. (IPv6 addresses are 128 bits.)

networking-technology.question: In full-duplex communication:
options: data flows in one direction only
data flows in both directions but only one at a time
data flows in both directions simultaneously
networking-technology.explanation: Full duplex allows simultaneous two-way transmission, like a telephone call. Half duplex alternates directions; simplex is one-way only.

networking-technology.question: HTTP is a protocol used mainly for:
options: transferring web pages between servers and browsers
networking-technology.explanation: HyperText Transfer Protocol defines how browsers request and servers deliver web resources such as HTML pages.

networking-technology.question: Data transfer rate (bandwidth) of a network is commonly measured in:
networking-technology.explanation: Network speeds are expressed in bits per second (bps) and its multiples — kbps, Mbps, Gbps.

instruction-set-8085.question: The instruction MOV A, B in 8085 assembly does what?
options: moves the content of A into B
moves the content of B into A
instruction-set-8085.explanation: In 8085 mnemonics, the destination is written first: MOV A, B copies the value from register B into register A, leaving B unchanged.

instruction-set-8085.question: The instruction MVI is used to:
options: load an 8-bit immediate value directly into a register
instruction-set-8085.explanation: MVI (Move Immediate), e.g. MVI A, 05H, loads the literal 8-bit constant that follows directly into the specified register.

instruction-set-8085.question: The instruction ADD B performs:
instruction-set-8085.explanation: ADD B adds the content of register B to the accumulator A, storing the result back in A; flags are updated accordingly.

instruction-set-8085.question: The instruction ADC differs from ADD in that ADC also:
options: adds the existing carry flag value into the sum
instruction-set-8085.explanation: ADC (Add with Carry) includes the current CY flag in the addition, essential for adding multi-byte numbers.

instruction-set-8085.question: The JMP instruction in 8085 is used to:
options: jump only if zero flag is set
unconditionally transfer control to a specified address
call a subroutine and save the return address
instruction-set-8085.explanation: JMP loads the given 16-bit address into the Program Counter unconditionally, unlike conditional jumps (JZ, JNZ, JC, etc.).

instruction-set-8085.question: The CALL instruction, unlike JMP, additionally:
options: pushes the return address onto the stack before jumping
instruction-set-8085.explanation: CALL saves the address of the next instruction on the stack so that a matching RET can resume execution there after the subroutine finishes.

instruction-set-8085.question: PUSH and POP instructions in 8085 operate on:
options: register pairs (16-bit), transferring data to/from the stack
instruction-set-8085.explanation: PUSH B, PUSH D, PUSH H, PUSH PSW transfer 16-bit register pairs to the stack; matching POP instructions retrieve them, adjusting SP by 2 each time.

instruction-set-8085.question: The instruction CMP B compares register B with the accumulator by:
options: internally subtracting B from A and setting flags, without changing A
instruction-set-8085.explanation: CMP performs A - B internally to set the zero and carry flags for a subsequent conditional jump, but the actual value of A is left unchanged.

instruction-set-8085.question: The mnemonic for the instruction that increments the content of the accumulator by 1 is:
instruction-set-8085.explanation: INR A increases the accumulator's value by one; its counterpart DCR A decrements by one.

instruction-set-8085.question: STA and LDA instructions are used respectively to:
options: store and load the accumulator to/from a direct memory address
set and clear the carry flag
instruction-set-8085.explanation: STA addr copies the accumulator's content to the specified memory address; LDA addr loads the accumulator from that address.

intel-x86-family.question: The Intel 8086 microprocessor is a:
intel-x86-family.explanation: The 8086 has 16-bit registers and a 16-bit data bus, a major advance over the 8-bit 8085.

intel-x86-family.question: The 8086 architecture is internally divided into two functional units, namely:
options: the Bus Interface Unit (BIU) and the Execution Unit (EU)
intel-x86-family.explanation: The BIU handles memory/IO access and instruction fetching, while the EU decodes and executes instructions — allowing overlapped, pipelined operation.

intel-x86-family.question: The 8086 has a 20-bit address bus, giving it a maximum addressable memory of:
intel-x86-family.explanation: 2²⁰ = 1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB, computed via physical address = (segment × 16) + offset.

intel-x86-family.question: Segmentation in 8086 memory addressing is used to:
options: divide the 1 MB memory into logical segments (code, data, stack, extra)
eliminate the need for an address bus
intel-x86-family.explanation: Segment registers (CS, DS, SS, ES) combined with an offset let the 16-bit architecture address the full 1 MB space in manageable blocks.

intel-x86-family.question: Compared to the 8086, the 80386 processor introduced:
options: true 32-bit architecture with paging and virtual memory support
intel-x86-family.explanation: The 80386 was Intel's first 32-bit x86 processor, adding a 32-bit data bus, paging unit, and protected-mode virtual memory.

intel-x86-family.question: The 8086 can operate in two modes: minimum mode and maximum mode, distinguished mainly by:
options: whether the system supports a single processor (minimum) or multiprocessor configuration (maximum)
intel-x86-family.explanation: Minimum mode configures the 8086 for simple, single-CPU systems; maximum mode enables coprocessor/multiprocessor support via an external bus controller.

intel-x86-family.question: Pipelining, as used from the 8086 onward, improves performance by:
options: executing one instruction at a time with no overlap
overlapping the fetch and execute stages of successive instructions
intel-x86-family.explanation: While the EU executes one instruction, the BIU can simultaneously fetch the next, reducing idle time and increasing overall throughput.

intel-x86-family.question: Real mode operation of x86 processors restricts addressable memory to:
intel-x86-family.explanation: Real mode is a backward-compatible mode where later x86 chips behave like the 8086, limited to the 1 MB segmented address space.

intel-x86-family.question: Protected mode, introduced with the 80286 and enhanced later, primarily provides:
options: memory protection and multitasking support between programs
intel-x86-family.explanation: Protected mode enforces memory access boundaries between processes and enables virtual memory and multitasking, essential for modern operating systems.

intel-x86-family.question: The general trend in the evolution from 8086 to modern x86 processors includes:
options: decreasing word size and transistor count
increasing word size, clock speed, and the addition of cache memory and multiple cores
removal of the instruction set entirely
intel-x86-family.explanation: Successive x86 generations grew from 16-bit to 64-bit, gained on-chip cache, multiple cores, and vastly higher clock speeds while retaining instruction-set compatibility.
